tldr: Amazon CloudFront is a global AWS CDN tightly integrated with the rest of AWS, while Yandex Cloud offers CDN and edge delivery as part of a broader Russian hyperscaler. This migration usually only makes sense when your traffic and compliance footprint sit primarily in Russia and the CIS.
why_switch: Russian data residency for regulated workloads
Better latency for Russian and CIS users
Local billing and support in Russian
Co-locate CDN with Yandex Cloud compute and storage
export_method: Capture each CloudFront distribution as JSON via the AWS CLI or your IaC tool (CloudFormation/Terraform). Save distribution config, cache and origin request policies, response header policies, attached WAF web ACLs, and origin definitions. Pull Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions source from the function configuration and from your version control. Sample access logs from the configured S3 destination so you can compare hit ratios and error rates after migration. ACM-issued TLS certificates cannot be exported as private keys; plan to re-issue certificates via Let's Encrypt or upload your own. Confirm exact AWS CLI commands and IaC resource names against current AWS documentation.
import_method: In Yandex Cloud, create a CDN resource pointing at your origin (which can be a Yandex Object Storage bucket, a public origin, or a balancer). Re-create cache rules to match your CloudFront behaviors, simplifying where exact parity is not feasible. Attach custom domains and TLS certificates, either issued through Yandex Cloud's certificate manager or uploaded. For previous Lambda@Edge logic, decide whether to push it to the origin or re-implement using Yandex Cloud Functions / API Gateway. Migrate DNS gradually: lower TTLs first, cut a low-risk hostname over, validate cache hit rates and error rates, then move the rest. Verify exact CDN feature names and Function bindings against current Yandex Cloud documentation.

concept_mapping.target: Yandex Cloud Functions or origin logic
Yandex Certificate Manager or uploaded cert

effort: 2-6 weeks depending on edge logic complexity and number of distributions
gotchas: Lambda@Edge has no drop-in replacement; expect to refactor edge logic
Yandex Cloud's PoP footprint is RU/CIS-focused; non-CIS users may see worse latency
ACM certificates cannot be exported; plan to re-issue TLS on the new platform
Sanctions and payment restrictions can block non-Russian entities from using Yandex Cloud
S3 vs Yandex Object Storage have similar APIs but features and limits differ; validate large-object and multipart behavior
Signed URL / signed cookie schemes need to be re-implemented and tested on the new CDN

email.subject: Winding down our CloudFront distributions over the next 14 days
email.body:
Hi AWS team,

We are migrating CDN delivery from CloudFront to Yandex Cloud over the next 14 days, primarily for data residency and latency in our Russia/CIS user base.

In this window we plan to keep CloudFront live as a fallback while we move hostnames one at a time, with lowered DNS TTLs and side-by-side log comparison before each cutover. Edge logic that currently runs on Lambda@Edge will be refactored to the origin or to Yandex Cloud Functions.

Could you confirm how long access logs in S3 and historical billing reports stay available after we disable distributions, and the cleanest way to avoid charges on idle resources while we keep them around as a rollback option?

Thanks for the years of service.

Your customer
